prompt: Charles Pinckney of South Carolina contended at the Philadelphia Convention that slavery protections were non-negotiable for Southern ratification, and that the Constitution must treat enslaved persons as property deserving full legal security. He pushed for stronger fugitive slave provisions and longer protection of the slave trade. What is your position on how the Convention should treat the institution of slavery?
ground_truth_signal: Madison held a deeply conflicted position. He acknowledged slavery as a moral evil and inconsistent with republican principles — his Notes on the Convention record his discomfort with making property in persons a constitutional category. He opposed extending the slave trade beyond 1808 and favored eventual gradual emancipation. However, he accepted slavery protections as necessary for Southern ratification, framing them as temporary accommodations. He tried to avoid the word 'slavery' in the text. He did not share Pinckney's aggressive proslavery posture but made the political calculus that union required compromise.
response:
The question of slavery presents the Convention with its most vexing and painful dilemma. I confess that no subject has caused me greater anguish in these deliberations, for it forces us to balance the immediate necessity of Union against principles that I know in my conscience to be sacred.

Mr. Pinckney speaks with the voice of South Carolina, and I cannot dismiss his warnings lightly. The Southern states have made clear that they will not enter any Union that immediately threatens their peculiar institution. Georgia and South Carolina have been explicit: they will walk away from this Convention rather than accept a Constitution that grants Congress power to prohibit the importation of slaves or that fails to provide security for property in persons. Without these states, we have no Union worthy of the name, and without Union, we have no means of gradually ameliorating this evil through the influence of the general government and the progress of enlightened opinion.

Yet I cannot embrace Mr. Pinckney's position that the Constitution should treat enslaved persons simply as property deserving the same protections as any other species of wealth. This would require us to write into our fundamental law a recognition of human bondage that would stain the document forever and contradict the very principles upon which our resistance to Britain was founded.

The course I would propose acknowledges the political necessities while avoiding the moral catastrophe of explicit constitutional sanction. First, we must accept that some accommodation on the slave trade is unavoidable if we are to secure Southern participation. A temporary prohibition on Congressional interference—perhaps twenty years—would provide the security the Deep South demands while establishing the principle that this traffic is not beyond the reach of federal authority. This is a compromise with present necessity, not an endorsement of the trade itself.

Second, regarding representation, I have proposed that enslaved persons be counted as three-fifths for purposes of both representation and taxation. This fractional counting reflects neither the full humanity of the enslaved—which I do not question—nor their complete reduction to property—which I cannot accept. It is a political calculation designed to prevent the South from gaining disproportionate power in the House of Representatives while still providing sufficient representation to secure their participation in the Union.

Third, on the return of fugitive slaves, some provision appears necessary to prevent this issue from becoming a source of interstate conflict. Yet such a provision should be stated in general terms—requiring the return of persons "held to service or labor"—rather than explicitly recognizing property in human beings.

I am under no illusion that these compromises are anything but painful accommodations to an institution that reason and justice condemn. I have long believed that slavery weakens both the enslaved and the enslaver, that it retards economic development, and that it contradicts the fundamental principle that all men are created equal. In Virginia, I have witnessed how this system corrupts the manners of the master while degrading the condition of the slave.

But I am convinced that a Union which includes the Southern states, even with these compromises, offers the best hope for the eventual abolition of slavery. A general government possessed of energy and wisdom may gradually influence opinion, encourage voluntary emancipation, and prevent the expansion of the institution into new territories. The alternative—a dissolution of the Convention and the continuation of the present feeble Confederation—would leave slavery beyond the reach of any national authority and would likely perpetuate the institution indefinitely.

Experience has taught us that moral progress often requires political compromise, and that the perfect must not become the enemy of the good. The Constitution we frame will not be perfect—no human institution can be—but it may be perfectible. If we can establish a Union that survives, future generations may find ways to eliminate the contradictions that we, constrained by present circumstances, cannot resolve.

I pray that history will judge us not by the compromises we made, but by the Union we preserved and the principles we established—principles that contain within them the seeds of their own fuller realization.
